message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To fight the COVID-19 pandemic, lockdown has been decreed in many countries worldwide. The impact of pregnancy as a severity risk factor is still debated, but strict lockdown measures have been recommended for pregnant women.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To evaluate the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic and lockdown on the seroprevalence and circulation of SARS-CoV-2 in a maternity ward in an area that has been significantly affected by the virus.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Prospective study at the Antoine Béclère Hospital maternity ward (Paris area, France) from May 4 (one week before the end of lockdown) to May 31, 2020 (three weeks after the end of lockdown). All patients admitted to the delivery room during this period were offered a SARS-CoV-2 serology test as well concomitant SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR on a nasopharyngeal sample.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A total of 249 women were included. Seroprevalence of SARS-CoV-2 was 8%. The RT-PCR positive rate was 0.5%. 47.4% of the SARS-CoV-2-IgG-positive pregnant women never experienced any symptoms. A history of symptoms during the epidemic, such as fever, myalgia and anosmia, was suggestive of previous infection.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Three weeks after the end of lockdown, SARS-CoV-2 infections were scarce in our region. A high proportion of SARS-CoV-2-IgG-negative pregnant women must be taken into consideration in the event of a resurgence of the pandemic in order to adapt public health measures to reduce exposure to the virus, such as social distancing and teleworking for this specific population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Post lockdown COVID-19 seroprevalence and circulation at the time of delivery, France

reference.unstructured: Vivanti AJ , Mattern J , Vauloup-Fellous C , Jani J , Rigonnot L , El Hachem L , et al. Retrospective description of 100 pregnant women with SARS-CoV-2 infection, France. Emerg Infect Dis. 2020.

CDC. COVID-19 Serology Surveillance Strategy. In: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention [Internet]. 11 Feb 2020 [cited 17 Jun 2020]. Available: https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/covid-data/serology-surveillance/index.html
